practices.title: Always quote variable expansions
practices.explanation: Unquoted variables undergo word splitting and glob expansion, enabling injection when variables contain spaces or special characters.

practices.title: Never hardcode secrets in shell scripts
practices.explanation: Hardcoded secrets in shell scripts are visible in process listings and version control.

practices.title: Use arrays for commands — avoid eval and word splitting
practices.explanation: eval and unquoted expansion enable arbitrary code execution. Use bash arrays to safely construct commands.

practices.title: Validate input before using in file paths or commands
practices.explanation: Unsanitized input in paths enables directory traversal (../) attacks. Validate against an allowlist pattern.

practices.title: Use functions to organise scripts
practices.bad: # 200 lines of linear script with no structure
practices.explanation: Linear scripts are hard to debug and test. Functions provide named scopes and enable selective testing.

practices.title: Use meaningful variable names — avoid single letters
practices.bad:
for f in $(ls *.txt); do
    x=$(wc -l < "$f")
done
practices.good:
for filename in ./*.txt; do
    line_count=$(wc -l < "${filename}")
done
practices.explanation: Single-letter variables make scripts unreadable. Use descriptive names that convey purpose.

practices.title: Keep scripts under 200 lines — extract functions or split files
practices.explanation: Long scripts are hard to debug and test. Extract reusable logic into functions or sourced helper scripts.

practices.title: Run ShellCheck on all scripts
practices.bad: # No linting — bugs caught in production
practices.explanation: ShellCheck catches quoting bugs, POSIX issues, and common mistakes automatically. Run it in CI.

practices.title: Use trap for cleanup on exit
practices.explanation: Without trap, early exits and errors leave temp files behind. trap EXIT ensures cleanup on all exit paths.

practices.title: Check return codes — use set -euo pipefail
practices.explanation: Without error checking, commands silently fail and subsequent commands run in wrong state.

practices.title: Validate required arguments at script start
practices.good:
if [[ $# -lt 1 ]]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <input-file>" >&2
    exit 1
fi
INPUT_FILE="$1"
practices.explanation: Missing argument validation causes cryptic errors downstream. Check required args early with clear usage messages.

practices.title: Use mktemp for temporary files — never hardcode paths
practices.explanation: Hardcoded temp paths create race conditions and symlink attacks. mktemp generates unique, safe filenames.

practices.title: Avoid useless cat — pipe directly or use redirection
practices.explanation: Piping cat into another command adds an unnecessary process. Most commands accept file arguments directly.

practices.title: Use built-in string operations instead of external commands
practices.explanation: Bash built-in parameter expansion is faster than spawning sed/awk. Use ${var#}, ${var%}, ${var//} for simple string ops.

practices.title: Avoid subshells in loops — use while-read or built-ins
practices.bad:
for f in $(find . -name '*.log'); do
    count=$(wc -l < "$f")
done
practices.good:
find . -name '*.log' -print0 | while IFS= read -r -d '' f; do
    count=$(wc -l < "$f")
done
practices.explanation: $(find ...) in for loops breaks on spaces and forks a subshell. Use find -print0 | while read for safe iteration.

practices.title: Pre-compute values outside loops
practices.bad:
for i in $(seq 1 100); do
    today=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
    echo "${today}: item ${i}"
done
practices.good:
today=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
for i in $(seq 1 100); do
    echo "${today}: item ${i}"
done
practices.explanation: Calling external commands inside loops spawns a process per iteration. Move invariant computations outside the loop.
